standardLabel: Decode multisyllable words using word parts
title: Grade 3 Multisyllable Word Parts Lesson
lessonExplanation: When a word has more than one syllable, break it into smaller parts. Read each part, then blend the parts back together.
workedExample: The word helpful can be read as help + ful. The ending -ful means full of, so helpful means full of help or useful.

choices: care/ful/ly
ca/ref/ully
caref/ul/ly
carefu/lly
guidedPractice.passage: The class found a smooth stone beside the stream. Later, they carefully wrote notes about the shape, color, and size of each object they collected.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which division helps read the word carefully?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Care/ful/ly shows meaningful word parts that help with reading and meaning.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: care/ful/ly

standardLabel: Combine main ideas and details across texts
title: Grade 4 Combine Main Ideas Across Texts Lesson
lessonExplanation: When two texts are about related topics, compare the main idea of each text. Then decide what bigger idea both texts help explain.
workedExample: One text may explain how wetlands protect animals, while another explains how wetlands reduce flooding. Together, the texts show that wetlands help both wildlife and communities.

choices: Both texts explain ways students can help the environment.
Both texts are mostly about lunch menus.
One text is fiction and one has no facts.
The texts disagree about planting seeds.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which answer best combines the main ideas of two texts about school gardens and composting?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The answer combines the shared environmental idea from both topics.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Both texts explain ways students can help the environment.

standardLabel: Determine main ideas and summarize key details
title: Grade 5 Main Idea Lesson
lessonExplanation: Main idea is what the paragraph, section, or whole passage is mostly about. Grade 5 target: Summarize main ideas across sections or related texts. Text complexity: Longer passage or paired short texts with more than one important section. Evidence demand: Use multiple details from different parts of the text. Reasoning: Synthesize important ideas and leave out minor details.
workedExample: If two related texts describe mulch and rain barrels, a strong summary combines the shared idea: conservation improves when people use more than one water-saving method.

choices: Different conservation methods can work together to protect a garden.
One class studied only rain barrels.
Dry weather makes gardens impossible.
The project was mostly about carrying buckets.
guidedPractice.passage: Two classes studied ways to protect the school garden during dry weather. One group tested mulch and found that covered soil lost less moisture. Another group studied rain barrels and learned that stored rainwater reduced the need for extra hose water. Taken together, the projects showed that conservation works best when students combine daily habits with tools that save resources.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Summarize main ideas across sections or related texts.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use multiple details from different parts of the text.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "Different conservation methods can work together to protect a garden." because it meets this Grade 5 expectation: Write or select a summary that combines the central idea with the most relevant details.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Different conservation methods can work together to protect a garden.

standardLabel: Analyze how a central idea is developed
title: Grade 6 Central Idea Development Lesson
lessonExplanation: In Grade 6, central idea work moves beyond naming the topic. Students track how an idea is introduced, refined, and supported across multiple paragraphs.
workedExample: If an article begins with a problem, adds data from a study, and ends with a community solution, the central idea is developed through problem, evidence, and response.

choices: By introducing a problem, giving evidence, and explaining a response
By listing unrelated facts about the topic
By repeating the title in each paragraph
By ending with a personal opinion only
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which answer best explains how the author develops the central idea?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Grade 6 students should explain the movement of the idea across the text, not just identify the topic.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: By introducing a problem, giving evidence, and explaining a response

choices: By introducing a problem, giving evidence, and explaining a response
By listing unrelated facts about the topic
By repeating the title in each paragraph
By ending with a personal opinion only
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided Check: Which answer best explains how the author develops the central idea?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Grade 6 students should explain the movement of the idea across the text, not just identify the topic.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: By introducing a problem, giving evidence, and explaining a response

choices: By introducing a problem, giving evidence, and explaining a response
By listing unrelated facts about the topic
By repeating the title in each paragraph
By ending with a personal opinion only
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.

guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Grade 6 students should explain the movement of the idea across the text, not just identify the topic. This item adds another chance to apply the same skill with the Grade 6 evidence expectation.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: By introducing a problem, giving evidence, and explaining a response

standardLabel: Analyze central idea development across sections
title: Grade 7 Central Idea Across Sections Lesson
lessonExplanation: In Grade 7, students analyze how a central idea grows across sections of a text. Track how the author introduces the idea, complicates it, and uses evidence to refine it.
workedExample: An article may begin by defining a problem, then use statistics, expert explanation, and an example to show why the problem is more complex than it first appears.

choices: The author introduces a concern, adds data that complicates it, and ends by explaining a possible response.
The author mentions the topic once and then changes subjects.
The author repeats the title without adding evidence.
The author gives only a personal opinion.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which answer best analyzes how a central idea develops across the text?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Grade 7 central idea analysis should explain how sections work together to develop the idea.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The author introduces a concern, adds data that complicates it, and ends by explaining a possible response.

standardLabel: Analyze central idea development and objective summary
title: Grade 8 Main Idea Lesson
lessonExplanation: Main idea is what the paragraph, section, or whole passage is mostly about. Grade 8 target: Evaluate how central ideas are refined by evidence, structure, and purpose. Text complexity: Complex informational text with layered claims, counterpoints, or author emphasis. Evidence demand: Use precise evidence tied to structure and purpose. Reasoning: Evaluate how the author refines or complicates the central idea.
workedExample: A Grade 8 answer evaluates refinement: the text begins with water conservation, then refines that idea into evidence-based community problem solving.

choices: The central idea develops from saving water to using evidence for broader community problem solving.
The text argues that tools alone solve every problem.
The text mostly lists unrelated school supplies.
The central idea stays simple and unchanged.
guidedPractice.passage: A student research committee studied whether a redesigned schoolyard could serve both environmental and instructional goals. The report first argues that water conservation depends on measurable systems, such as rain collection and drip irrigation. It then refines that claim by showing that systems alone are not enough: students must interpret data, revise routines, and consider maintenance over time. The central idea develops from a simple conservation goal into a broader argument about evidence-based community problem solving.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Evaluate how central ideas are refined by evidence, structure, and purpose.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use precise evidence tied to structure and purpose.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "The central idea develops from saving water to using evidence for broader community problem solving." because it meets this Grade 8 expectation: Explain how evidence, structure, and purpose work together to refine central ideas.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The central idea develops from saving water to using evidence for broader community problem solving.

choices: The central idea develops from saving water to using evidence for broader community problem solving.
The text argues that tools alone solve every problem.
The text mostly lists unrelated school supplies.
The central idea stays simple and unchanged.

The central idea develops from a simple conservation goal into a broader argument about evidence-based community problem solving.

exitTicket.passage: A student research committee studied whether a redesigned schoolyard could serve both environmental and instructional goals. The report first argues that water conservation depends on measurable systems, such as rain collection and drip irrigation. It then refines that claim by showing that systems alone are not enough: students must interpret data, revise routines, and consider maintenance over time. The central idea develops from a simple conservation goal into a broader argument about evidence-based community problem solving.
